county_metadata.record_losses: No catastrophic losses; some early Spanish-era records deteriorated.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of nine original NM Territory counties (1852). Albuquerque founded 1706. Sandoval County carved out 1903. Major population center of NM.
county_metadata.research_tips: Bernalillo County is New Mexico's most populous and holds rich genealogical resources spanning Spanish colonial, Mexican, and American periods. The Archdiocese of Santa Fe parish records for San Felipe de Neri Church in Old Town Albuquerque date to the 1700s and are on FamilySearch microfilm. The Center for Southwest Research at UNM holds manuscript collections, land grant papers, and oral histories critical for Hispanic families. County Clerk has deeds from 1852; Probate Court records are separate. Check SANM I & II at SRCA for colonial-era families in the Albuquerque area.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Index and images via county clerk; older records at SRCA

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed and conveyance records from territorial organization. Land grants predate county system.

source_profiles.source_name: San Felipe de Neri Parish Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Baptisms, marriages, burials for Albuquerque's founding parish. Some of NM's oldest surviving church records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Microfilmed by FamilySearch; originals with Archdiocese

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses from territorial period. Earlier marriages in Catholic parish registers.
Wills, estates, guardianships. Spanish-era testaments at SRCA.
Federal census 1850-1950. Spanish colonial censuses (padrones) at SRCA for earlier periods.

source_profiles.source_name: Albuquerque Town Grant & Atrisco Land Grant
source_profiles.repository: SRCA / BLM / Center for Southwest Research
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish and Mexican land grants in Bernalillo area. Town grant and surrounding community grants document original settler families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Surveyor General and Court of Private Land Claims files at SRCA

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Territorial-era newspapers including both English and Spanish language press. Legal notices, obituaries, probate notices.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1921 from Socorro County. Named for Thomas B. Catron, NM's first US Senator. Largest county by area in NM. Sparsely populated — ranching and forestry.
county_metadata.research_tips: Catron County is New Mexico's largest by area but one of its least populated, making records sparse but focused on ranching and homesteading families. Pre-1921 records are held by Socorro County. The Gila National Forest covers much of the county; USFS records and grazing permits can identify early ranching families. Reserve, the county seat, has the County Clerk's office with deeds from 1921. Mogollon mining district records (gold/silver, 1890s-1920s) are valuable for tracing miners. Check FamilySearch for microfilmed probate and marriage records. SRCA in Santa Fe holds territorial-era court records for the Socorro County precinct areas that became Catron.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: In-person only at County Clerk, Reserve

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Pre-1921 deeds in Socorro County.
Marriage records from 1921. Earlier marriages under Socorro County.
Wills and estate records from 1921.

source_profiles.source_name: BLM General Land Office — Catron County Patents
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents for homesteads in the Catron County area.
Small rural cemeteries in Reserve, Glenwood, Mogollon, and surrounding communities.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1889 from Lincoln County. Named for J. Francisco Chaves, NM territorial delegate. Roswell is county seat — Pecos Valley agricultural center. Roosevelt County carved out 1903.
county_metadata.research_tips: Chaves County centers on Roswell and the Pecos Valley, settled primarily by Anglo-American cattlemen and farmers from the 1870s onward, with an established Hispanic community as well. Pre-1889 records are in Lincoln County (seat: Carrizozo). County Clerk in Roswell has deeds from 1889. The Historical Society for Southeast New Mexico in Roswell holds manuscripts, photographs, and oral histories. Roswell was a military town — Walker Air Force Base (formerly Roswell AAF) records may document military families. Chaves County ranching brands at NM Livestock Board. Catholic and Protestant church records supplement civil records. Check the Chaves County Genealogical Society for compiled family files.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Online index available; images require in-person visit

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Land records from county formation. Pecos Valley irrigation and agricultural land transfers.
Marriage licenses from 1889.
Wills, estates, guardianships from 1889. Cattle ranching estates common.
Federal census 1900-1950. Pre-1889 enumerated under Lincoln County.

source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / Chaves County Genealogical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: South Park Cemetery (Roswell), Hagerman, Dexter, Lake Arthur cemeteries.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1981 from Valencia County — NM's newest county. Named for the legendary Seven Cities of Cibola. Contains Acoma Pueblo, Laguna Pueblo, and parts of Ramah Navajo Chapter. Grants was a uranium mining center.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cibola County was carved from Valencia County in 1981, so all pre-1981 records remain in Valencia County (seat: Los Lunas). For genealogical research predating 1981, begin at the Valencia County Clerk. Grants boomed as a uranium mining center in the 1950s-1970s; mining company employment records and Atomic Energy Commission documentation can help trace workers. Acoma Pueblo ('Sky City') and Laguna Pueblo are within county boundaries — tribal enrollment and BIA records are essential for Pueblo families. Catholic mission records at Acoma (San Esteban del Rey, est. 1629) and Laguna are among the oldest in the US. The NM Mining Museum in Grants has local history materials.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Pre-1981 deeds in Valencia County.

source_profiles.source_name: San Esteban del Rey & Laguna Mission Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Mission registers for Acoma and Laguna Pueblos. Among the oldest church records in the American West.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Microfilmed; some gaps due to Pueblo Revolt (1680)

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses from 1981. Pre-1981 marriages in Valencia County.

source_profiles.repository: NARA Denver / BIA Southern Pueblos Agency
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Census rolls, allotment records, and agency correspondence for Acoma and Laguna Pueblos.
source_profiles.survival_notes: At NARA Denver and Albuquerque BIA office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Pre-1981 area enumerated as Valencia County. Includes Pueblo Indian schedules.

county_metadata.record_losses: Partial loss of early records in 1880s courthouse fire at Cimarron; most records reconstructed.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1869 from Mora and Taos counties. Original county seat was Cimarron, moved to Springer 1882, then Raton 1897. Union County carved out 1893. Maxwell Land Grant — largest private land grant in Western Hemisphere — covers much of county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Colfax County history revolves around the Maxwell Land Grant, which at 1.7 million acres was the largest private land holding in the Western Hemisphere. The Colfax County War (1870s-1880s) over grant lands generated extensive court records now at SRCA. Cimarron was the original county seat — some early records lost when the seat moved, but most were reconstructed. Mining towns of Elizabethtown, Baldy, and the Raton coal mining district produced employment and vital records. The Atchison, Topeka & Santa Fe Railway ran through Raton Pass; railroad employment records trace many families. Catholic parish records at Cimarron, Springer, and Raton supplement civil records. Arthur Manby's land records and the Maxwell Land Grant Company papers are at the NM Highlands University library.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from 1869. Maxwell Land Grant conveyances are extensive.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some early records reconstructed after 1880s Cimarron fire

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of the Maxwell Land Grant including original Mexican grant, Lucien Maxwell's consolidation, and subsequent litigation through the Court of Private Land Claims.
Marriage licenses from territorial period.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Intact from Raton era; some Cimarron-era gaps

source_profiles.coverage_notes: District court records including Colfax County War cases, land grant disputes, and mining litigation.
Federal census from 1870. 1860 area enumerated under Mora/Taos.
Territorial-era newspapers covering mining, ranching, and land grant litigation.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1909 from Quay and Roosevelt counties. Named for George Curry, NM territorial governor. Clovis is county seat — a railroad town on the ATSF line near the Texas border.
county_metadata.research_tips: Curry County was created during the railroad homesteading boom on New Mexico's eastern plains. Clovis was founded in 1907 as an ATSF railroad division point, drawing workers from across the US. County Clerk in Clovis holds deeds from 1909; pre-1909 records split between Quay (Tucumcari) and Roosevelt (Portales) counties. Cannon Air Force Base (formerly Clovis Army Air Field, WWII) records document military families stationed there. The Clovis-Curry County Chamber has historical files. Eastern NM counties have more Anglo-American settlement patterns than older Hispanic counties to the west. Clovis Depot Model Train Museum has some railroad genealogy resources. Check Protestant church records, especially Baptist and Methodist.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Railroad-era homestead transfers.
Marriage licenses from 1909.
Wills, estates from county formation.
WWII-era Clovis Army Air Field and Cold War Cannon AFB personnel records.
Federal census from 1910 (first after formation). Pre-1909 area under Quay/Roosevelt.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1917 from parts of Chaves, Guadalupe, and Roosevelt counties. Named for Ezequiel Cabeza de Baca, NM's second governor. Fort Sumner is county seat — site of Billy the Kid's death and Bosque Redondo.
county_metadata.research_tips: De Baca County is one of NM's least populated counties, centered on Fort Sumner. The Bosque Redondo Memorial at Fort Sumner preserves the history of the Navajo and Mescalero Apache internment (1863-1868) — the Long Walk. Billy the Kid was killed by Pat Garrett here in 1881. Pre-1917 records are split among Chaves (Roswell), Guadalupe (Santa Rosa), and Roosevelt (Portales) counties. County Clerk in Fort Sumner has deeds from 1917. Ranching families dominate the record base. Fort Sumner State Monument and the Billy the Kid Museum have local history materials. Catholic parish records from Fort Sumner and nearby communities at the Archdiocese of Santa Fe. BLM GLO records for homestead patents in the area.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: In-person at County Clerk, Fort Sumner

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Pre-1917 records split among parent counties.
Marriage licenses from 1917.
Wills and estates from formation.
Fort Sumner Cemetery (includes Billy the Kid grave site), rural ranching cemeteries.
Federal census from 1920 (first after formation).

county_metadata.record_losses: Some early records damaged or lost during Civil War occupation (1861-1862).
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: One of nine original NM Territory counties (1852). Las Cruces founded 1849. Otero and Sierra counties carved out. Border county — Mesilla was briefly Confederate Arizona Territory capital 1861-1862. Mesilla was part of Mexico until Gadsden Purchase (1854).
county_metadata.research_tips: Dona Ana County straddles the US-Mexico border and the Mesilla Valley, making cross-border research essential. Mesilla was the county seat before Las Cruces and was part of Mexico until the Gadsden Purchase (1854). The Rio Grande Historical Collections at NMSU in Las Cruces hold manuscript collections, ranch records, and oral histories. Catholic parish records from San Albino Church in Mesilla (1852) and Las Cruces parishes are on FamilySearch. Confederate occupation records (1861-1862) at SRCA document the brief period under the CSA. County Clerk has deeds from 1852. Mexican civil and church records in Ciudad Juarez may be needed for families predating the border change. Fort Selden and Fort Fillmore military records at NARA document frontier families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Online index; images available at clerk's office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed records from territorial organization. Mesilla Valley land transactions extensive.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Minor gaps from Civil War era; mostly intact

source_profiles.source_name: San Albino & Las Cruces Parish Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish records for Mesilla Valley communities. San Albino in Mesilla from 1852.
Marriage licenses from territorial period.
Wills, estates, guardianships from 1852.
Federal census 1850-1950. Mesilla Valley enumerated from earliest territorial census. Includes both English and Spanish surname families.

source_profiles.source_name: US-Mexico Border Crossing Records — El Paso District
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Border crossing manifests for the El Paso district covering Dona Ana County crossings. Essential for Mexican immigrant families.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1889 from Lincoln County. Originally named 'Eddy County' for Charles B. Eddy, railroad promoter. Carlsbad (originally Eddy) is county seat. Carlsbad Caverns National Park. Potash and oil industries.
county_metadata.research_tips: Eddy County in southeastern NM was developed through irrigation and railroad promotion by Charles B. Eddy in the late 1880s. Carlsbad (originally named Eddy) drew settlers from Texas and the Midwest. County Clerk in Carlsbad has deeds from 1889; pre-1889 records under Lincoln County (Carrizozo). The Carlsbad Museum and Art Center has local history collections. Oil and potash industry employment records from the 1920s onward trace many working families. Lea County was carved from Eddy in 1917. The Southeastern NM Historical Society has compiled family files. Catholic and Protestant church records supplement civil records. Eddy County Genealogical Society maintains local indices.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deeds from county formation. Irrigation company land transfers prominent.
Marriage licenses from 1889.
Wills, estates from 1889.
Federal census 1900-1950.

source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / Eddy County Genealogical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Carlsbad Cemetery, Artesia Woodbine Cemetery, and rural cemeteries.
